---
title: "Projects"
---

My research examines how environmental constraints, institutions, and public
policies shape production, land use, and development in low- and middle-income
countries. It combines microeconometric methods with field surveys,
administrative data, environmental data, and cross-country datasets.

## ECOTERM: Ecosystem services of termite mounds in Cambodia

[ECOTERM](https://cerdi.uca.fr/version-francaise/recherche/projets-de-recherche/ecoterm#/)
is an interdisciplinary research project on the ecosystem services provided by
termite mounds in agricultural landscapes in Cambodia. It brings together
economists, ecologists, anthropologists, botanists, and soil scientists.

Within the project, I lead the economics work package. Our work investigates
how termite mounds affect rice production, farmers' input use, land allocation,
and rural livelihoods. We combine repeated farm and plot-level surveys with
ecological information to understand whether termite mounds act as productive
assets, biodiversity reservoirs, or constraints for agricultural households.

Current work includes research on termite mounds and rice yields, as well as a
study of their implications for input allocation and agricultural
misallocation.

**Related work**

- Lao, C., M. Audibert, P. Jouquet, R. Muon, K. Sok, and S. Marchand.
  *Beyond the Surface: When Termite Mounds Affect Rice Production in Cambodia?*
  Submitted manuscript.

---

## Agriculture and rural environmental change in China

This research programme examines the sustainability of agricultural production
in China, with a particular focus on family farming, input use, environmental
performance, and the consequences of industrial development for rural areas.

My work has studied environmental efficiency in conventional and non-certified
organic rice farming, social interactions and input spillovers among family
farmers, and the effects of industrial water pollution on rice production. It
shows that environmental performance depends not only on the production
technology itself, but also on farmers' experience, local interactions, and
exposure to external environmental pressures.

**Selected publications**

- Marchand, S. and H. Guo (2014). [The environmental efficiency of
  non-certified organic farming in China: A case study of paddy rice
  production](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chieco.2014.09.006). *China Economic
  Review*, 31, 201--216.

- Guo, H. and S. Marchand (2019). [Social interactions and spillover effects
  in Chinese family farming](https://doi.org/10.1080/00220388.2018.1443206).
  *The Journal of Development Studies*, 55(11), 2306--2328.

- Marchand, S., M. Barro, H. Xiong, and H. Guo (2025). [Industrial water
  pollution and farmer adaptation: Evidence from rice farming in
  Jiangsu, China](https://doi.org/10.1111/agec.12867). *Agricultural
  Economics*, 56(1), 108--123.

---

## Media, air pollution, and environmental governance in China

This project studies the role of information and public attention in
environmental regulation. It asks whether media coverage can act as an informal
mechanism of accountability by influencing citizens, local governments, and
firms.

Using daily data on media sentiment, air quality, weather, and industrial
activity across Chinese cities, this research examines how the emotional tone of
environmental news is associated with pollution outcomes. Current extensions
focus on social media discussions and on firms' emissions behaviour.

**Selected publication**

- Marchand, S., D. Cubizol, E. Nasho Ah-Pine, and H. Guo (2025). [Mass media
  as an environmental watchdog? Evidence from the war on air pollution in
  China](https://doi.org/10.1017/S1355770X25100284). *Environment and
  Development Economics*, 1--25.

**Current work**

- Marchand, S., D. Cubizol, and H. Guo. *Sentiment Analysis and Air Pollution:
  Evidence from Weibo Posts in China.*

- Marchand, S., D. Cubizol, and H. Guo. *Sentiment Analysis and Air Pollution:
  Evidence from Industrial Firms in China.*

---

## Forests, land use, and forest-based development

My research on forests builds on my doctoral work on institutions and
deforestation in developing countries, as well as on my postdoctoral experience
in Brazil.

In 2012, I was a postdoctoral researcher at the Federal University of Viçosa,
working in collaboration with the Institute for Applied Economic Research
([IPEA](https://www.ipea.gov.br/)) in Rio de Janeiro. I worked with
[José Gustavo Feres](https://epge.fgv.br/pt/professor/jose-gustavo-feres) as
part of the [Rede Amazônia Sustentável](https://ras-network.org/) (RAS;
Sustainable Amazon Network), an interdisciplinary initiative led by
[Toby Gardner](https://www.sei.org/people/toby-gardner/) on the social and
environmental dimensions of land-use change in the Brazilian Amazon.

This line of research has addressed the relationship between agricultural
productivity and deforestation, conservation incentives for local governments,
the persistence of low-income and environmentally degrading land uses, and the
effects of log export bans on wood processing and employment in developing
countries.

**Selected publications**

- Marchand, S. (2012). [The relationship between technical efficiency in
  agriculture and deforestation in the Brazilian
  Amazon](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2012.02.025). *Ecological
  Economics*, 77, 166--175.

- Sauquet, A., S. Marchand, and J. G. Feres (2014). [Protected areas, local
  governments, and strategic interactions: The case of the ICMS-Ecológico in
  the Brazilian state of Paraná](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2014.09.008).
  *Ecological Economics*, 107, 249--258.

- Garrett, R. D., T. A. Gardner, T. F. Morello, S. Marchand, J. Barlow,
  D. Ezzine de Blas, J. Ferreira, A. C. Lees, and L. Parry (2017).
  [Explaining the persistence of low income and environmentally degrading land
  uses in the Brazilian Amazon](https://doi.org/10.5751/ES-09364-220327).
  *Ecology and Society*, 22(3).

- Marchand, S. and M. Zerbo (2023). [Revisiting the trade
  restrictions-industrialization nexus in developing countries: The case of log
  export ban and wood processing](https://doi.org/10.1561/112.00000562).
  *Journal of Forest Economics*, 38(2), 195--233.

- Marchand, S. and M. Zerbo (2025). [Enhancing employment through log export
  ban: Insights from wood-processing firms in developing
  countries](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.forpol.2025.103491). *Forest Policy and
  Economics*, 174, 103491.

---

## Climate risks, vulnerability, and adaptation

A more recent part of my research examines how climate risks affect production
systems and how public policies can support adaptation in developing countries.

This work includes research on the effects of extreme climate events on
agricultural efficiency in Vietnam, the measurement of physical vulnerability
to cyclonic risks, the valuation of insurance against low-probability risks,
and the allocation of international adaptation finance.

**Selected publications and chapters**

- Mankaï, S., S. Marchand, and N. H. Le (2024). [Valuing insurance against
  small probability risks: A meta-analysis](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.socec.2024.102181).
  *Journal of Behavioral and Experimental Economics*, 109, 102181.

- Goujon, M. and S. Marchand (2023). *Measuring Vulnerability to Climate
  Change.* In F. Cafarelli (ed.), *The Resilience of Territories Exposed to
  Natural Hazards*. Mare & Martin. [in French]

- Goujon, M., S. Marchand, and O. Santoni (2023). *A Comparative Measure of
  Territorial Vulnerability to Cyclone Activity: The Case of Vietnam.* In
  R. Bentirou Mathlouthi and A. Pomade (eds.), *Environmental
  Vulnerabilities: Interdisciplinary Perspectives*. L'Harmattan. [in French]

**Current work**

- Beaucoral, P., M. Goujon, and S. Marchand. *No Plan, No Aid? The Effects of
  National Adaptation Plan Implementation on Received Adaptation Aid.*
  
**Other work**

- Diallo, Y., S. Marchand, and E. Espagne. *Impacts of Extreme Climate Events
  on Technical Efficiency in Vietnamese Agriculture.*

For a broader account of these research themes, see my
[HDR thesis](hdr.qmd).
